Description: Massive fortress with wall and moat
Description: Daemonic exaltation
Description: Shackled deity of nature
Description: Collection of [[1D4]] ziggurats
Description: Crystalized Corruption
Description: Hero cult
Description: Imperial pleasure palace
Description: Member of the imperial or royal family
Description: Monster display

1-2.description:
Weak Poison
Requires a passed [Strong –3] test to break loose, and triggers an alarm which is heard across the entire level. Disarming it requires a [Cunning –3] test and a [Discreet –3] test in order to do so quietly.

3-3.description:
Moderate Poison
Requires a passed [Strong –3] test to break loose, and triggers an alarm which is heard across the entire level. Disarming it requires a [Cunning –3] test and a [Discreet –3] test in order to do so quietly.

4-4.description:
Strong Poison
Requires a passed [Strong –3] test to break loose, and triggers an alarm which is heard across the entire level. Disarming it requires a [Cunning –3] test and a [Discreet –3] test in order to do so quietly.

1-2.description: The Loremaster ability or Elf: directions to a troll forge, either active or long burnt out and abandoned.

3-4.description: The Loremaster ability or Goblin: warning of some nearby danger, still present or long gone. Use a threat from this list or create your own.

5-6.description: The Loremaster ability or Troll: directions to a “song cliff,” which could either be a name for an ancient assembly site for trolls, a magic node, or both. Whether it is still being used or has any power left is up to the Gamemaster.

1-1.description: Base camp for an Ambrian expedition. [[1d6]] Ambrians and [[1d10]] helpers in a crude log house.

2-2.description: Initiation site for witches. [[1d4]] witches have gathered in this cave to initiate [[1d6]] future witches into the mysteries of witchcraft.

3-3.description: Goblin settlement. [[1d10*5]] goblins of various ages occupy this stalactite, which hangs like an inverted tower from the cave ceiling. A rope ladder is lowered whenever the goblins want to go up or down.

4-4.description: Troll dwelling. A sturdy door, guarded by a Gwann, leads to half a dozen cave chambers where [[1d10+10]] trolls live under the leadership of a liege troll. They are reserved, but not hostile, and willing to trade thaler for quality metalworks.

5-5.description: Refuge for the blight marked. A village made up of simple huts huddled in a cave chamber. It is inhabited by [[1d6+10]] blight marked humans and goblins, led by a severely stigmatized woman named Akulina. They are afraid of everyone, and terrified of being blight born. Villagers who come alarmingly close to that fate are burned in a nearby cave.

6-6.description: Undead. A small settlement with [[1d4+2]] undead humans (both Ambrians and barbarians). They are led by the undead theurg Tamas, who stubbornly clings to Prios’ forgiveness, despite the fact that sunlight burns his puss-­filled eyes so badly that he never goes outside during daytime. At night he brings his flock to the surface to pray and lament their bitter fate.

1-1.description: The Ordo Magica lead the expedition,

2-2.description: The Sun Church lead the expedition,

3-3.description: An Ambrian noble family lead the expedition,

4-4.description: Treasure hunters lead the expedition,
@Actor[7bFPMzVmyT9JN1bE]{Spite}

5-5.description: @Actor[JKOjhppHC7hnkpZD]{Gwann Slaughterer}

6-6.description: @Actor[kZIV5h6dODMRwEsg]{World Serpent Tunneler}

1-2.description: Pool of bubbling blackness sometimes shooting up like a pitch black geyser (roll [[1d6]] per round, 1 = eruption). Whoever touches the blackness suffers [[1d6]] temporary Corruption, as does anyone present in the cave when the geyser erupts unless they pass a Quick test. On success the individual only suffers 1 point of temporary Corruption.

3-4.description: Black flakes whirling in the dark, often chaotically, sometimes almost like a coordinated dance. Anyone walking through the cave suffers [[1d4]] temporary Corruption.

5-6.description: Throbbing boils in the rock wall that crack open whenever something living passes by, splashing them with sticky Corruption. Those walking through the cave must pass three Discreet tests or trigger one boil per failure. For each boil that cracks, the affected must pass a [Quick +3] test or suffer [[1d4]] temporary corruption.

1-2.description:
Weak Poison
Breaking loose requires a successful [Strong −3] test

3-3.description:
Moderate Poison
Breaking loose requires a successful [Strong −3] test

4-4.description:
Strong Poison
Breaking loose requires a successful [Strong −3] test

1-2.description: Ambrian expedition: a week’s worth of food for five people, [[1d6]] blankets, [[1d4]] lanterns and lamp oil, and [[1d6]] herbal cures.

3-4.description: Monster hunter: various trophies on drying racks, together worth [[1d6*100]] thaler.

5-5.description: Iron Pact: waybread for [[1d10*10]] days, [[1d6]] quivers with 20 arrows each, [[1d4]] fur bedrolls, [[1d4]] lanterns with lamp oil, and [[1d4]] doses of moderate antidote.

6-6.description: Goblins: food for [[1d10+10]] people in the form of dried insects, mushrooms and pieces of obscure meat. Leaves to sleep on for [[1d10]] goblins, [[1d4]] daggers, and a large jar of foul-­tasting, but strong, liquor.

4-4.description: Hallucinogenic mushrooms, [[1d4]] doses, causes wild daydreams which are paralyzing unless the consumer passes a Strong test (Poisoner, Witchcraft).

5-5.description: Corrupting mushrooms, [[1d4]] doses, inflicts [[1d4]] temporary corruption (Poisoner, Theurgy).

6-6.description: Corruption cleansing mushrooms, [[1d4]] doses, removes [[1d4]] temporary corruption (Medicus, Theurgy).

1-2.description: Rune-­carved monolith. The Loremaster ability: pre-­Symbarian remains. A group who studies the runes carefully for [[1d4]] hours gains 1d4 Experience from their insights into the world prior to the rise of Symbaroum.

3-4.description: Monster temple. The Loremaster ability: Symbarian cult site. Those who study the temple carefully for [[1d4]] days gain [[1d12]] Experience and 1 permanent corruption for their insight into Symbaroum’s dark history. They also find cult items worth [[1d10×100]] thaler. At some point during the search, one of the old “gods” slumbering underneath the temple wakes up; choose any strong monster – it does not have to belong in the Underworld.

5-5.description: Barbarian tower. The Loremaster ability or barbarian: the tower is a barbarian creation carved with protective witch symbols. Those who spend a day studying the tower and its protective symbols gain [[1d4]] Experience, and find arm rings and other barbarian ornaments worth [[1d6×10]] thaler. However, they also attract (PC+1) predators of weak or ordinary resistance.

6-6.description: Village of Ambrian freethinkers. Obvious to everyone: a number of log houses and a palisade have collapsed and been completely demolished. Those who spend [[1d4]] hours searching the rubble find [[1d6]] Ambrian settlers of different ages, all dead and carrying [[1d10]] ortegs each. A successful Vigilant test also reveals a hidden cache containing [[1d10]] thaler and a journal detailing the settlers’ exploits in the depths of Davokar. Studying the books grants the gaming group [[1d4]] Experience through their understanding of the hard life in the woodland gloom. The journal is also worth [[1d10]] thaler to scholars in Ambria.

1-2.description:
Circle of worshipers, all but one with their throats cut. In front of the last person lies a dagger and an empty bowl of poison.
The bodies are completely petrified, and mineralized tears shine like pearls on their cheeks. [[1d10]] of these tear pearls can be picked loose.
They are worth [[1d10*10]] thaler each, and absorb one point of temporary Corruption each, being destroyed in the process.

3-4.description:
A line of humans who were first walking, then crawling, toward some distant refuge.
This is as far as they got.
Their bodies are mummified, resembling ancient wood.

5-5.description:
A family huddled together in a shallow hole in the ground, incinerated in the middle of a final, everlasting embrace.
Their bodies are ash, and the slightest touch will send them crumbling into dust.

6-6.description:
Scattered bodies, trapped by death in various unnatural positions, covered by gently falling dust.
Actually a group of PC+7 waiting @UUID[Actor.gQoycsBHlvKDs1QN]{Dragoul}.

1-2.description: A memory cube containing an ancient man’s memories from a happy or sad occasion, a baptism, a wedding or a funeral. Those who share the experience gain [[1d4]] Experience.

3-4.description: A blackened cranium still whispering prayers to long dead gods. Whoever listens to its prayers for [[1d4]] hours either gains [[1d12]] Experience or loses one point of permanent corruption.

1-2.description: The throne stands alone in the desolate wasteland, its prince long gone. It is made from parts of a capsized stone ship. Those who study the throne for [[1d4]] hours gain the same amount of Experience from their new understanding of ancient human ingenuity.

3-4.description: The throne is empty, but surrounded by daemonic supplicants awaiting the prince’s return. The throne is made of living bodies – pact-making humans who displeased their master. Those who talk to the supplicants and study the throne gain dark insights worth [[1d12]] Experience, and one point of permanent corruption.

5-6.description:
A daemon prince holds court, surrounded by dancing daemons.
Humans nailed on black monoliths provide the music.
All who hear the lamentations of the tormented humans must pass a Resolute test or gain [[1d6]] temporary corruption.
Player characters without permanent corruption are not affected.

The daemon prince demands a pact with at least one of the player characters, or else he will unleash the daemons on them.

The throne is roughly made from the linked remains of human buildings; beams of rusty steel, a huge and highly patinated brass bell, a rose window of multi-colored glass.

A gaming group who studies the throne gains deep insights into humanity’s origin and early history, which grants [[1d12]] Experience.

2-2.description: Vengeful Terrain

4-4.description: @UUID[Actor.3XO4JdpuJ3h2Sg26]{Blight Worm}

5-5.description: @UUID[Actor.9lthwpzHVj6NZDGb]{Glimmer}

6-6.description: @UUID[Actor.GoMkJXd7ub3TaPWj]{Primal Blight Beast}

4-4.description: @UUID[Actor.k3RD29CBc1xVvrVn]{Necromage}

5-5.description: @UUID[Actor.8XM0ztoQ1eTras8a]{Nightmare}

6-6.description: @UUID[Actor.If5R786jSreSfEFP]{Cryptwalker}

4-6.description:
Underworld Event:
Vigilant test; a hidden door leading to a deserted hideout.
Variants/Details:

7-8.description:
Underworld Event:
Mushroom patch (bracketed ability can be used to understand what the specific mushroom is and what it does).
Variants/Details:

9-10.description:
Underworld Event:
Collapsed ruin. A sink­hole has lived up to its name and pulled a building down into the Underworld. The sky can be seen high above, and mighty trees lean alarmingly far over the hole.
Variants/Details:

11-12.description:
Underworld Event:
Outpost. An established and reasonably thriving colony down in the dark.
Variants/Details:

17-17.description:
Underworld Event:
Rune-­covered gate.
To open the gate one must first solve its riddle [Cunning –3].
Details:
The gate leads to a Cryptlord’s court, active or abandoned. If the Cryptlord is still around, so are [[1d10]] @UUID[Actor.gQoycsBHlvKDs1QN]{Dragoul} resting in their alcoves. In that case there is also an artifact in here, as well as minor treasure worth [[1d10*10]] thaler. If the place is abandoned one can only find the treasure.

18-18.description:
Underworld Event:
The tunnel of a World Serpent.
It is 10 meters in diameter and seems likely to collapse
Details
Roll [[1d20]] every hour; if the result is less than or equal to the number of hours spent in the tunnel then it collapses.
Quick tests are needed to avoid [[1d12]] damage (ignores Armor).
Those who take damage must roll over the damage on [[1d20]] or get stuck in the rubble.
It takes [[1d4]] hours to clear the rubble and free those who are stuck.

19-19.description:
Underworld Event:
The remains of an Ambrian expedition, scattered over the cave floor.
Details
[[1d10*10]] thaler can be found on the bodies, as well as a handwritten letter describing how well the expedition is going.
Someone with the Beast Lore ability can determine what kind of monster wiped out the expedition – choose from the Monster Encounter event or whatever else seems appropriate.

20-20.description:
Underworld Event:
Crystal column, ice blue with streaks of purple light. Loremaster or Mystical Tradition: it is a smaller magic node not yet completely destroyed by corruption.
Details
This node devours all temporary corruption generated in its vicinity, and grows increasingly dark as a result.
Once it becomes utterly black (relevant ability and a successful Cunning test: the node can take another [[1d20+100]] corruption), the crystal cracks and everyone in the cave chamber gains [[1d10]] temporary corruption.
As long as the node exists, beasts (both predators and prey) will be attracted to the area and abominations and undead are prevented from entering.
Thoroughly corrupted creatures can enter, but take [[1d4]] damage per turn (ignores Armor), and these points affect the node as temporary corruption.

Table 5 - Corruption in the Yonderworld.name: Table 5 - Corruption in the Yonderworld
Table 5 - Corruption in the Yonderworld.description:
Corruption in The Yonderworld

* Note that suffered temporary corruption does not fade away until the victim arrives at a place without corrupting nature

1-2.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Dead node of cracked black crystal, with Corruption oozing from the gaps
Details
The corrupted node no longer absorbs any temporary Corruption, but mystics can choose to gain Corruption from the node’s smoking cadaver and gain one of the following advantages as a bonus action on their turn, gaining (1 temporary Corruption) each time:
  • +1 on one success test per accepted corruption (max +3 on a particular test).
  • +1 level on an effect die per accepted corruption (from 1d6 to 1d8, up to 1d12).
  • Recover [[1d4]] Toughness per accepted corruption (max [[3d4]] per turn).

5-6.description:
Yonderworld Event:
A find in the wasteland. Whoever passes a Vigilant test finds one of the following:

7-8.description:
Yonderworld Event:
The great throne of a daemon prince, alone in the desert
Variants/Details:

11-11.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Teleporting demonologist
Details
A demonologist is engaged in combat in the normal world when she plane shifts to the Yonderworld to run a bit before finally jumping back into the fight on “the other side.” The demonologist has stats as a @UUID[Actor.k3RD29CBc1xVvrVn]{Necromage}, if the player characters join the fray.

12-12.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Blackened temple
Details
A once white and shiny temple dedicated to long since forgotten gods is now blackened and haunted by daemons.

A gaming group who searches the temple for [[1d4]] hours gains [[1d12]] Experience from dark insights, as well as [[1d4]] temporary corruption.

They also find an artifact (see below).

Furthermore, their search awakens a daemon or abomination of challenging resistance who attacks the player characters.

13-13.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Shipwreck of stone
Details
A stone ship has run aground and capsized in the sea of dust.
Everyone on board is either dead or blight born.

A gaming group who searches the giant vessel gains insights worth [[1d12]] Experience and finds strange and valuable items worth [[1d10*100]] thaler.

They are also attacked by two waves of thoroughly corrupted sailors, stats as @UUID[Actor.gQoycsBHlvKDs1QN]{Dragoul} (PC+5 in each wave).

14-14.description:
Yonderworld Event:
A sandstorm approaches. PCs must find shelter from the wave of corruption.
Details
Finding a cavity that offers enough shelter takes a successful Vigilant test; the characters then take [[1d4]] temporary corruption as the storm blows by.

Those trapped in the storm gain
[[1d8]] temporary corruption
and take [[1d4]] damage from the suffocating sand.

15-15.description:
Yonderworld Event:
The ground turns into treacherous mud
Details
It takes a successful Vigilant test to notice this; the person who goes first and fails the test gets stuck in the mud.
Pulling him/her up requires three successful Strong tests.

If all three fail, the victim sinks into the mud and drowns.
Powers or abilities that enable movement through the ground or underwater can save one’s life, but the blighted mud still inflicts [[1d4]] temporary corruption.

16-16.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Pointy rooftops of copper protrude from the ground
Variants/Details
The player characters find themselves on the roof of a huge building, buried in deep and thick mud.

Those who spend [[1d4]] hours digging finally reach the top floor and find an artifact (see below).

Frescoes provide clues about human history worth [[1d12]] Experience.

Anyone digging this way inside the building gains [[1d6]] temporary corruption.

17-17.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Doomsday clock of rusty iron
Details
The mechanical clock has stopped at two minutes to midnight.

A player character with the ability Artifact Crafting or Blacksmith can salvage the clockwork from the clock itself and make it tick again.

It then works as a mechanical version of the artifact @UUID[Item.uwwMnEfrSjEzRq7N]{Xtala's Hourglass}.

18-18.description:
Yonderworld Event:
Interrupted aqueduct that no longer carries water, but thick, blighted mud.
Details
With a successful Vigilant test, the player character finds a raft of glass up on the aqueduct, covered with dust.

The character can use it to ride the river of mud at double walking speed, but the course is set by the aqueduct.

19-19.description:
Yonderworld Event:
A beach of skulls and bones by a vast sea of fuming acid.
Details
As the player characters approach the beach, something moves underneath the surface, creating ripples on the acid’s otherwise mirror-like surface.

The Alchemy ability: a melee weapon or projectile for a ranged weapon dipped in the lake will forever deal –2 damage, but gains the quality Acid Coated.

A person who disturbs the calm surface of the lake (e.g. dipping a weapon into it) is attacked by an abomination with stats as @UUID[Actor.yge7WufmDDCmZ5OA]{Hammer Eel}, but thoroughly corrupted).

20-20.description:
Yonderworld Event:
A glass pyramid protrudes from the ground, its interior teeming with movement.
Details
Further investigation reveals that the pyramid is full of blight born humans, fighting to break free from their glass prison. Seeing the player characters increases their frenzy, and a famished roar is heard ever so faintly through the thick glass.

Table 7 - Events In The Spirit World.name: Table 7 - Events In The Spirit World

1-1.description:
Spirit World Event:
A lonesome soul walks sadly, but resolutely, toward the edge of Eternity. Whoever interrupts the wanderer must pass a Resolute test or start walking with him.

Others must pass a Persuasive test for the player character to come to her senses and stop seeking eternal oblivion.

2-2.description:
Spirit World Event:
A group of souls, large and small, are walking together.

The large ones wait for the little ones to catch up and take their hands before stepping into Eternity together.

Whoever witnesses the harmonious event loses 1 point of permanent Corruption.

3-3.description:
Spirit World Event:
A solitary soul is waiting for someone that never comes.

It turns listlessly around and goes alone into the void. Whoever disturbs the wanderer must defend themselves against a @UUID[Actor.z1E351dB0FXZxrAC]{Wraith}.

4-4.description:
Spirit World Event:
A swarm of tiny flares drift toward the edge of Eternity.

Perhaps they are the spirits of a flock of beasts or a cloud of insects?

Those who take a moment to reflect on the event gain [[1d4]] Experience.

5-5.description:
Spirit World Event:
A spirit is searching around the border to the world of the living, looking for someone who did it harm.

person who knows the ritual Necromancy can now communicate with the spirit without using the ritual.

The spirit tells the character who it is looking for, and asks for her help in seeking revenge.

A person who helps the spirit – slaying the offender in the living world – either gains [[1d12]] Experience or loses one point of permanent corruption once the deed is done.

6-6.description:
Spirit World Event:
A dreamer in the world of the living has lost her way and stands, surprised and amazed, in the borderland between life and death.

Then the vision flutters and dissipates as the dreamer wakes up.

Anyone present who witnesses the dissipation can try to Possess the dreamer’s body in the living world, as if using the ritual with the same name.

7-7.description:
Spirit World Event:
A spirit is shouting silently across the boundary between life and death; a hand reaches in from the side of the living, and for a brief moment the two lovers touch each other’s fingers.

Then the deceased is pulled back into the Spirit World, and the cycle begins anew.

Anyone witnessing this may try to help the two lovers hold hands a little longer; it requires a successful Resolute test.

On success the deceased relaxes, kissing its lover’s hand.
Before wandering off into Eternity, the spirit kisses the cheeks of everyone who helped.

Those who helped either lose a Burden, gain [[1d10]] Experience, or lose one point of permanent corruption.

8-8.description:
Spirit World Event:
A spirit is obsessively building a structure of ashes, only to see it collapse again and again.

A person who helps the spirit finish the structure must pass a Quick test.

In that case, the spirit gives its creation a satisfied nod, bows to the player characters, and begins its walk to Eternity.

The characters gain [[1d4]] Experience.

9-9.description:
Spirit World Event:
A spirit is digging in the ashes for some lost item.

Player characters who decide to help out must pass a Vigilant test, in which case they find an artifact (see below) in the ashes.

The deceased throws itself at the item, and anyone trying to stop the spirit must fight a @UUID[Actor.k3RD29CBc1xVvrVn]{Necromage}.

If the undead is defeated, the characters may keep the artifact.

10-10.description:
Spirit World Event:
Monster Encounter
The party encounters a:

1-3.description:
Event:
Cursed
Description:
A missionary claims a deer gave him the evil eye, causing concern among the whole expedition.
Effect:
All participants make a Resolute test; those who fail suffer –1 to all success tests for 24 hours.

4-5.description:
Event:
Punished
Description:
A missionary claims to have dreamed that another member’s thoughts have displeased Prios, overwhelming everyone with shame and guilt.
Effect:
All participants make a Resolute test; those who fail suffer –2 to all success tests for 24 hours.

6-6.description:
Event:
Infected
Description:
A missionary gets an impulse to pick berries from an unknown bush and offer them around – likely not from Prios, but from some false god.
Effect:
Everyone is exposed to a Weak disease.

7-7.description:
Event:
Unlucky
Description:
It is as if the sun no longer shines on the group; nothing seems to be going right.
Effect:
During the next scene, an odd result on a success test means failure, except if the result is 1.

8-8.description:
Event:
Prios’ Judgment
Description:
The expedition leader sees the fingers of Prios (sunrays) pointing at a body of water and encourages everyone to drink from it.
Effect:
Everyone is exposed to a Strong disease.

12-14.description:
Event:
Blessed
Description:
A missionary hears Prios’ voice whispering words of love in the wind rustling through the trees.
Effect:
All participants make a Resolute test; a success adds +1 to all success tests for 24 hours.

15-16.description:
Event:
Sanctified
Description:
An expedition member wakes up with a never-­before-­heard hymn to Prios stuck in their head.
Effect:
Singing the hymn grants everyone +2 to all success tests for 24 hours.

17-17.description:
Event:
Sun Temple
Description:
The expedition comes across an ancient, overgrown ruin; there is a sun symbol carved above the entrance.
Effect:
It takes a full day to explore the ruin, which turns out to be a nest of hungry spiders (of suitable resistance).

18-18.description:
Event:
The Wellspring of the One
Description:
The group passes a wellspring where a few points of light glitter despite it being in the shade.
Effect:
[[1d8]] drops of natural @UUID[Item.ZIjseCjEtqNLOvXa]{Water of Dusk} are drifting around in the spring; collecting them requires one successful Vigilant test per drop; failure causes the drop to burst and mix with the water.

19-19.description:
Event:
Revelation
Description:
A dazzling golden-­white figure descends through the foliage and touches all missionaries (but no companions) on the forehead with a finger.
Effect:
Those it touches may choose one benefit: 
  • Gain 10 Experience
  • Lose [[1d4]] permanent corruption
  • Lose one Burden
  • Gain +1 to all success tests or effect tests for one month
  • Gain a sacred protection that lasts for one month and allows the person to ignore 1 damage from all attacks, or 2 damage from the natural weapon of abominations or undead

20-20.description:
Event:
Divine Gift
Description:
The group stumbles upon the decayed remains of an arach; all equipment has long been destroyed, save for a single object.
Effect:
Prios has left a gift for the missionaries, in the form of an artifact
(the Game Master chooses one
from Artifacts).

1-5.description:
Description: Out of battle
Enemies:
There is no combat scene and the characters can use actions to drink elixirs, use healing spells, etc.
Effects
Gives –3 to the roll on Table 16: The Course of the Battle.

6-10.description:
Description: On the fringe of battle
Enemies:
The player characters encounter [PC number of] Weak opponents.
Effects
Winning does not modify the roll on Table 16: The Course of the Battle; losing gives –1.

11-15.description:
Description: In the midst of battle
Enemies:
The player characters encounter [PC+1] Weak opponents led by an Ordinary enemy.
Effects
Winning gives +1 to the roll on Table 16: The Course of the Battle; losing adds no modifier.

16-20.description:
Description: Critical point of the battle
Enemies:
The player characters encounter two waves of enemies; the first wave consists of [PC+1] Weak opponents led by an Ordinary enemy. The second wave follows immediately, with no rest in between, and consists of [PC] Ordinary opponents led by a Challenging enemy. The player characters may choose to retreat after the first wave, in which case the scene counts as a defeat.
Effects
Winning gives +3 to the roll on Table 16: The Course of the Battle; losing adds no modifier.

21-21.description:
Description: Moment of truth
Enemies:
The characters encounter three waves of enemies; the first wave consists of [PC] Ordinary opponents. The second wave follows immediately, with no rest in between, and consists of [PC+1] Ordinary opponents led by a Challenging enemy. Then there is a short break to use healing powers and drink elixirs. The final wave consists of an experienced officer/general plus bodyguards: [PC+1] Challenging enemies and one Strong enemy. The player characters may choose to retreat after the first or second wave, in which case the scene counts as a defeat.
Effects
Winning adds +5 to the roll on Table 16: The Course of the Battle; losing adds no modifier.

Table 15 - Fate Table for Pitched Battle.name: Table 15 - Fate Table for Pitched Battles

1-1.description:
Fate:
Dead from wounds
Effect:
The player character is left in the mud of the battlefield and dies from her wounds, once and for all.

2-2.description:
Fate:
Killed by enemy
Effect:
The player character could have made it, but an enemy appears and ends her life. The player character and the enemy may speak briefly before the killing blow falls.

3-3.description:
Fate:
Captured by the enemy
Effect:
The player character survives her wounds (Toughness 1), but is captured by the enemy. Other player characters must either come to her rescue or pay a ransom for her freedom.

4-4.description:
Fate:
Wake among the dead, looted
Effect:
The player character wakes up among the dead (Toughness 1), robbed of all money and obvious valuables, and may then act freely.

5-5.description:
Fate:
Wake among the dead, unlooted
Effect:
The player character wakes up among the dead (Toughness 1) and may then act freely.

6-6.description:
Fate:
Saved by allies
Effect:
The player character is saved by her allies (Toughness 1), and wakes up in the main force’s encampment.

1-4.description:
Misfortune: Deserters
Description:
One tenth of the troops decide to run away, adversely affecting morale.
Effect:
The commander must pass a Persuasive test with the Leader ability to instil courage in the troops; an equally large group leaves the army every day until the test succeeds.

5-7.description:
Misfortune: Stragglers
Description:
The column is divided, and the rearguard is attacked by a local pack of predators.
Effect:
One fifth of the troops are killed, which may be cause to recalculate the odds of the upcoming battle see
Odds of the Pitched Battle

8-10.description:
Misfortune: Miserable terrain
Description:
Faulty navigation sends the army marching through rough terrain (such as wetlands)
Effect:
Movement speed is reduced by half for [[1d6]] days, or [[1d4]] if the commander (or an advisor) with Bushcraft passes a Cunning test.

11-12.description:
Misfortune: Diseased water
Description:
An erroneous assessment of water quality causes stomach problems for a large chunk of the army
Effect:
A portion of the army is weakened for [[1d6]] days, or [[1d4]] days if a field medic (or equivalent) with Bushcraft passes a Cunning test:

13-14.description:
Misfortune: Mass desertion
Description:
A significant portion of the army refuses to continue unless their:

15-16.description:
Misfortune: Sabotage
Description:
Enemy agents set fire to the army’s supply of basic necessities, elixirs and medical equipment
Effect:
The army arrives on the battlefield weakened and poorly equipped, which results in a –2 modifier to rolls for
The Course of the Battle

17-18.description:
Misfortune: Infiltration
Description:
The enemy’s spies infiltrate the night camp and identify the player characters as the army’s leaders and toughest fighters
Effect:
The enemy’s leaders are warned to stay away from the player characters during the battle; the player characters have a –2 modifier when rolling for
Resistance per Combat Scene

19-19.description:
Misfortune: Nature’s revenge
Description:
Lack of concentration risks leading the army into an area with
Corrupted Nature

Effect:
The Commander and/or an advisor must pass two tests to quickly get the troops to safer terrain, first Vigilant with Bushcraft, then Persuasive with Leader.

If both tests fail, half of the troops die; one failure kills a quarter of them; if both succeed, only a tenth of the soldiers die.

20-20.description:
Misfortune: Primal blight beast
Description:
A huge monster of Mighty resistance suddenly appears in the army’s path and may cause soldiers to flee en masse.
Effect:
The Commander must (alone or with a group of confidants) engage the monstrosity within five turns. If she does not, or dies trying, the army will disperse as the soldiers flee in all directions.

4-4.description:
Encounter: Local settlement
Number: [[1d20+20]]
Modification for detection: +5

5-5.description:
Encounter: Rangers
Number: PC+2
Modification for detection: 0

6-6.description:
Encounter: Large expedition
Number: PC+[[1d6]]
Modification for detection: -1

8-8.description:
Encounter: Diplomatic elves, Civilized trolls, Peaceful bestials
Number: PC+[[1d10]]
Modification for detection: -5

9-10.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Weak
Example: Fortune Hunters, Jakaars, Frost Lights

11-12.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Weak, with Ordinary leader
Example: Robber chief + Robbers

13-14.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Ordinary
Example: Village Warriors, Kotkas, Blight-born Humans

15-16.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Ordinary, with Challenging leader
Example: Necromage + Dragouls

17-18.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Challenging
Example: Rage Trolls, Ferbers, Killer Shrubs

19-20.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Challenging, with Strong leader
Example: Lindworm + Aboars

21-22.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Strong
Example: Hunger Wolves, Colossi, Stone Boars

23-23.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Strong, with Mighty leader
Example: Skullbiter Queen + Skullbiter Crushers

24-24.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Mighty enemies, or PCx2 Strong
Example: Ravenous Willow old crushers, Primal Blight Beasts, Liege Trolls

25-25.description:
Encounter:
PC number of Mighty enemies, with Legendary leader
Example: The World Serpent + World Serpent wallowers

17-18.description:
Terrain:
Poisonous spores
Effect:
Everyone tests Strong. Failure causes 3 damage for 3 turns

19-20.description:
Terrain:
Vengeful terrain
Effect:
PC number of creatures, suiting the location

21-21.description:
Terrain:
Slightly corrupted nature
Effect:
One roll on
Corrupt Nature

22-22.description:
Terrain:
Corrupted nature
Effect:
Three rolls on
Corrupt Nature

23-23.description:
Terrain:
Severely corrupted nature
Effect:
Five rolls on
Corrupt Nature

Table 30 - Type of Item - Bright Davokar.name: Table 30 - Type of Item - Bright Davokar

Table 30 - Type of Item - Dark Davokar.name: Table 30 - Type of Item - Dark Davokar

Table 30 - Type of Item - Wild Davokar.name: Table 30 - Type of Item - Wild Davokar

1-2.description: Leather pouch containing stone marbles, fourteen shiny black ones and eight off-white, to play with or use as sling stones

3-4.description: Richly ornamented lyre of silver that can be fitted with new strings

5-6.description: Nail file with one rough side and one smooth, and a handle shaped like the silhouette of a wolf’s head

7-8.description: Box containing twenty-­four paper clips of patinated copper wire

9-10.description: Glass bottle containing a wooden model castle in a moorland landscape

11-12.description: Simple frying pan of rusted iron on which nothing ever sticks

13-14.description: Set of five bronze measuring cups, all in different sizes

15-16.description: Box containing thirty-­six small, bone discs engraved with Symbarian cuneiform characters

17-18.description: Small basket woven out of miraculously living vines with healthy green leaves

19-20.description: Necklace made entirely from linked gold teeth

21-22.description: Silver brooch depicting a wraith owl, the rings around its eyes made from black, faceted stones

23-24.description: White-glazed and somewhat cracked porcelain plate, painted with the weathered motif of a straight-­backed woman in a long dress, with a poleaxe resting against her shoulder

25-26.description: Comb made of lindworm bone with cuneiform characters on each tooth, which not only untangles one’s hair but cleans away dirt and grease

27-28.description: Cracked ceramic serving platter, decorated with various types of fruit on a white background – two of the fruits are unfamiliar to present-­day gardeners

29-30.description: Box in the shape of a rose with twenty-­four golden petals in four rows, which must be unfolded in sequence to expose its empty interior

31-32.description: Silver fountain pen with golden details, which never seems to run out of ink

33-34.description: Bronze caliper, marked in cuneiform with an unknown unit of measurement

35-36.description: Flowerpot of dark blue ceramic, decorated with a white and red motif of daemonic couples in obscene positions

37-38.description: Copper box containing twelve ceramic figurines, each depicting the same goblin’s head, but with different facial expressions

39-40.description: Patinated sheet of copper with cuneiform, listing the ingredients and procedure for brewing the alcoholic honey beverage Noid

41-42.description: Ornamented dagger with a dull steel blade that slides into the hilt when pressed against something

43-44.description: White-­glazed porcelain mask that covers the upper part of one’s face, shaped with an owl’s beak, small branched horns, and red-­lined eye openings

45-46.description: Wax stamp with a handle of pure gold whose mark represents the royal rune Labrys

47-48.description: Thick, cracked tallow candle which, when lit, emits a dark yellow smoke

49-50.description: Green, ceramic oil lamp with a shade of patterned glass that rotates while the lamp is lit

51-52.description: Brown, leather eyepatch with different-­colored gemstones arranged in the form of an open eye

53-54.description: Model of a twin-­masted stone ship; the stern can be opened to reveal sixteen tin figures spread across two decks

55-56.description: Black leather leash that connects to a collar of the same material, large enough to fit around a bull’s neck and decorated with studs of pure gold

57-58.description: Badly damaged wooden box with moldy fabric inside; contains five well-preserved seeds which, if handled correctly (successful Cunning with Alchemy), can grow into cherry trees with multicolored flowers and delicious fruit

59-60.description: Long, thin wooden box full of flat copper pieces of different shapes, all of them badly patinated; a puzzle with two missing pieces; washing off the patina reveals the motif of a daemonic abomination

61-62.description: Spoon of black bone, carved with runes filled with red paint that has partially worn off; nothing one eats with the spoon is absorbed by the body

63-64.description: 12 × 9 game board with different-­sized squares of various colors/materials (white bone, black wood, and patinated copper), in a frame of patterned hardwood

65-66.description: The clappers and tubes of a wind chime made from melodious ettermite

67-68.description: Fist-­sized, irregular, glistening black stone with magnetic properties

71-72.description: Carved bone statue of an andrik with a cracked beak, armed with a crossbow and a hatchet

73-74.description: Silver tuning fork in the key of D

75-76.description: Stiletto with a wavy blade of strange green metal; the hilt is shaped as a human child with clasped hands

69-70.description: Silver case with sixteen dried-up, multicolored pieces of chalk; they are unused and can be restored for drawing after successful alchemical treatment (Cunning test with Alchemy)

77-78.description: Statuette of an ox chiseled in black stone; raising its tail tilts the horns

79-80.description: Blackened walking stick, patterned like the scales of a snake or fish, with a fist-­sized pearl for a knob

81-82.description: Circular belt or cloak buckle made of silver, with gold details depicting three crossed arrows

83-84.description: Copper spyglass with rotating segments; aiming it at the sun reveals colorful patterns which change as one rotates the segments

85-86.description: Hourglass of misty white crystal contained within a blue-­gray metal stand, whose sand runs exactly twice as fast in one direction as it does in the other

87-88.description: Silver watering can whose long spout has been broken off; decorated with motifs of grotesque birds

89-90.description: Stuffed and mystically preserved ferret that has undergone a Symbarian rite of exaltation – equipped with predatory jaws, long claws, a crown of horns, and sickly yellow eyes

91-92.description: Bone flute, as long as one’s palm and as thin as a little finger; attracts [[1D20]] small birds if played by someone with the Musician boon

93-94.description: Shimmering pink pearl with streaks of red, the size of an eyeball, with the sweet-­sour taste of berry compote; never loses size or flavor

95-96.description: The parts of a broken dreamcatcher that can be repaired (Cunning test with Blacksmith). Brings peace to the sleeping; those burdened by Nightmares have two chances to pass their Resolute test

97-98.description: A clay cruse stoppered with a wax-­sealed cork, bearing a life-­sustaining seal that has kept its contents fresh for hundreds of years – red wine of excellent quality from a particularly superb vintage

99-100.description: Statue of glossy, dark gray stone, as tall as a forearm, in the form of a daemon. The base bears a cuneiform inscription that can be interpreted by someone with Loremaster (master): “Jeberaja”

1-1.description: Tall, thin vase of glazed porcelain, purple with a motif of bright red vines. Plants placed in the vase will not age, wither, bloom, or bear fruit.

2-2.description: Facial mask made of seashells in pink, white and light blue. Its wearer can hold their breath under water at a depth of up to 10 meters, for a maximum of ten minutes.

3-3.description: Handheld fan painted with stormy mountain scenery, which actively cools the air when waved. Roll [[1D6]] when used – a result of 6 inflicts [[1D4]] damage and causes facial hair to freeze.

4-4.description: Bronze bell whose inside and clapper is engraved with cuneiform characters. If carried so that it is hanging freely, it tinkles faintly whenever there is an abomination within a hundred meter radius, but at the risk of being detected [10←the creatures Vigilant].

5-5.description: Notebook with thin pages of parchment and pearl-­covered binding. Text written in the book is only visible to the person who wrote it.

6-6.description: Metal canister whose white color has largely peeled off. It is half full of a powder which conceals signs of aging on human skin for twenty-­four hours, at the cost of [[1d4]] temporary Corruption; contains enough powder for [[1d10+10]] applications.

7-7.description: Amulet set with a gray stone that emits a slimy secretion, which dries into an ineffective resin after a while. The secretion is enough for two individuals per scene. If applied on the skin, it has a repellent effect on insects; if the wearer succeeds with [13←Resolute] the insects select other targets.

8-8.description: Eight sticks of incense in a cylindrical leather case. Can be burned during negotiations, granting those with the Manipulator boon +2 to Persuasive tests.

9-9.description: Wedding ring of pure gold, adorned with six sapphires. A loving spirit is bound to the ring and can act in the wearer’s defense; once per scene the wearer may reroll a failed Defense test. But the spirit is jealous and will haunt the wearer’s romantic partner with horrifying nightmares.

10-10.description: Smooth wooden box containing two frameless spectacle lenses. Makes it easier to follow tracks in the wilderness (+1 to Vigilant tests), but weakens the other senses so that the wearer becomes less aware of her surroundings (–3 to Vigilant tests for detecting threats).

11-11.description: Three beautiful, cast-iron animal heads each the size of a clenched fist, completely free of rust – a wolf, a bear and a wild boar. Loops underneath allow them to be tied together into a bola, which adds +3 to the success test when thrown at creatures of the Beast category.

12-12.description: Long-stemmed briar pipe whose bowl is shaped like a woman’s face with her eyes closed. Can be used when performing the Oracle ritual, with its smoke conveying the visions, granting a +3 bonus to Resolute tests.

13-13.description: Lantern with red glass in a metal frame, about the size of a tankard. When lit in an otherwise dim environment, it can expose people who are trying to hide intense feelings (such as outrage, love or grief). It takes one successful Vigilant test per target to notice their seemingly glowing outlines.

14-14.description: A simple right-­handed chainmail gauntlet that needs relining. The person wearing it can never involuntarily lose their grip on their right-­hand weapon.

15-15.description: A modest ring of low-karat gold, with cuneiform runes engraved on the inside (“The cleansing one”). Its owner can conceal any skin damage for one scene per day, including scars and tattoos. These can still be detected with Witchsight and a successful Vigilant test.

16-16.description:
Key of blackened silver that can slide into most locks. Roll [[1d6]]: on
1–4: the lock opens;
5–6: the mechanism melts, surrounding the key and making it impossible to unlock.

17-17.description: Staff of smoke-colored crystal, as long as one’s palm, with one end shaped like a rounded point. When pointed at the North Star it gives a faint glow, granting +3 to Orientation tests, even underground.

18-18.description: Small copper pot, large enough to brew one dose of a single elixir. Adding an amount of silver equal to one (1) silver thaler makes the elixir one level stronger. If it is already Strong, the effect die is increased by one step.

19-19.description: Knife with a handle of scarlet wood and a ten centimeter curved blade, engraved with a running predator. Adds +2 to Cunning tests when harvesting a monster trophy.

20-20.description: Cracked, uncut ruby that emits enough heat to steam in cold and damp weather. If set in the hilt or grip of a one-handed weapon with the mystical quality Death Rune, the quality’s effect die is increased to [[1D10]], at the cost of [[1D4]] temporary corruption per turn.

3-3.description: @UUID[Item.vZxOm6nFWzzD33yD]{The Wraith Mark of the Eternity Legion}

4-4.description: @UUID[Item.MDzdRjABABkIm86s]{Feud Crystal}

5-5.description: @UUID[Item.q4teNj0ZJliRWchW]{Girakosh's Steel Circle}

6-6.description: @UUID[Item.yzMSNGDukYWfv4Qx]{The Living Chain of Hogarz}

10-10.description: @UUID[Item.eHf3T4HfT0PhWGsM]{The War Horn of the Stormhowler}

4-4.description: Function: Labyrinth

5-5.description: Function: Mine

6-6.description: Function: Temple

4-4.description: Untouched and ominously clean

5-5.description: Inexplicably cold/hot

6-6.description: Darkened (all light sources are diminished)

1-1.description: Corruptive. Temporary corruption gained inside the ruin is one Effect Die higher; 1 becomes 1d4, 1d4 becomes 1d6, and so on.

2-2.description: Sparse with treasure. All rolls for finding treasure have a second chance to fail.

3-3.description: Crowded. All rolls for the number of creatures in a room are made with 1d8 instead of 1d6

4-4.description: Desolate. All rolls for the number of creatures in a room are made with 1d4 instead of 1d6

5-5.description: Rich with treasure. All rolls for finding treasure have a second chance to succeed.

6-6.description: Lesser corruption. Temporary corruption gained inside the ruin is one Effect Die lower; 1d8 becomes 1d6, 1d6 becomes 1d4, and so on.

18-20.description: Make two rolls and keep both races; ignore further results of 18 or higher

2-2.description: The groups are unaware of each other.

3-3.description: The groups are aware of each other, but do not interact.

4-4.description: One group has been subjugated by the other.

5-5.description: The groups work together to an extent, but there is tension between them.

6-6.description: The groups work together harmoniously.

Table 39 - Entryways to Other Levels.name: Table 39 - Entryways to Other Levels
Table 39 - Entryways to Other Levels.description:
Entryways to Other Levels

*See Damage on Buildings, the Advanced Player’s Guide (page 106).

1-1.description: Intact stone staircase: difficult to destroy (Fortification 20, Breakpoint 5, Toughness 10)*

2-2.description: Intact wooden staircase: can be destroyed (Fortification 10, Breakpoint 5, Toughness 10)*

4-4.description: Destroyed staircase: replaced by rope ladder/rope bridge

5-5.description: Completely destroyed staircase

6-6.description: Hole in the ground/ceiling

7-7.description: Hole in the ground/ceiling, with a ladder which can easily be pulled up/down

3-4.description: Barricade: Attackers must spend an entire turn climbing to enter close combat. If in a hurry, a Quick test is required to get past it. Otherwise, doing so silently requires a Discreet test. Defenders can fire projectiles through or over the barricade without penalty, while attackers firing back at them have a –3 penalty on the success test.

5-5.description: Lone guard: A cultural being or guard beast tasked to raise the alarm in case of intruders.

6-6.description: Guard post: [PC –2] cultural beings, beasts, abominations or undead.

7-7.description: Trap: Can be detected with a [Vigilant –3] test, deals [[1d12]] damage plus:

8-8.description:
Lone guard: A cultural being or guard beast tasked to raise the alarm in case of intruders.
and
Barricade: Attackers must spend an entire turn climbing to enter close combat. If in a hurry, a Quick test is required to get past it. Otherwise, doing so silently requires a Discreet test. Defenders can fire projectiles through or over the barricade without penalty, while attackers firing back at them have a –3 penalty on the success test.

9-9.description:
Guard post: [PC –2] cultural beings, beasts, abominations or undead.
and
Barricade: Attackers must spend an entire turn climbing to enter close combat. If in a hurry, a Quick test is required to get past it. Otherwise, doing so silently requires a Discreet test. Defenders can fire projectiles through or over the barricade without penalty, while attackers firing back at them have a –3 penalty on the success test.

10-10.description:
Double Guard post: [PC –2] cultural beings, beasts, abominations or undead.
and
Barricade: Attackers must spend an entire turn climbing to enter close combat. If in a hurry, a Quick test is required to get past it. Otherwise, doing so silently requires a Discreet test. Defenders can fire projectiles through or over the barricade without penalty, while attackers firing back at them have a –3 penalty on the success test.

2-2.description: Wooden door, broken. Opening it unnoticed requires a Discreet test

4-4.description: Wooden door, intact, locked. Picking the lock quietly requires a Discreet test; can be kicked in with a Strong test

5-5.description: Reinforced wooden door, intact, unlocked, broken lock. Opening it unnoticed requires a Discreet test

6-6.description: Reinforced wooden door, intact, locked. Picking the lock quietly requires a Discreet test; cannot be kicked in, but can be destroyed with the right tools (Fortification 5, Breakpoint 5, Toughness 10)*

7-7.description: Copper or iron door, unlocked. Opening the door without it creaking loudly requires a successful Discreet test

8-8.description: Copper or iron door, locked and rusted/corroded shut. Picking its lock requires a successful Cunning test; then the door can be opened with a [Strong −5] test. The ensuing noise cannot be avoided

2-2.description: Creature: one individual of the group dominating the level

3-3.description: Group: [PC –2] creatures of the group which dominates the level

4-4.description: Crowd: [PC +2] creatures of the group dominating the level

5-5.description: Trap: can be detected with a [Vigilant −3] test, deals [[1d12]] damage plus:

6-6.description: Collapsing ceiling deals [[1d12]] damage, pass a Quick test to halve the damage, creates a hole in the ceiling

7-7.description: Collapsing floor 5 meters down, creates a hole in the floor

8-8.description: Remains: Fresh or old corpse/skeleton of a cultural being, wearing jewelry and/or coins worth [[1d10]] thaler

9-9.description: Wealthy remains: Fresh or old corpse/skeleton of a cultural being, wearing jewelry, items and/or coins worth [[2d100]] thaler

10-10.description: An item: The Game Master decides, or lets the players roll on Table 31 - Curiosities

11-11.description: Treasure trove: requires Vigilant test to be found; roll 1d4+1 times on Table 30 - Type of Item - Domain

Life Runs Out
No children are born alive in the location, harvests wither for no apparent reason, and the place soon becomes a miniature Alberetor, in all its lifeless desolation. Not even the wind seems to blow in the area; dust stirred by careless feet slowly floats to the ground.

12-12.description:
Increased Corruption
The ceremony has woken the vengeful wrath of the area, and the effect die for all actions which give the user temporary corruption is increased by one tier (from 1 to d4, d4 to d6, and so on). If the effect occurs where the ceremony is performed, this applies to the area outside the circle.

13-13.description:
Mystical Curse
The area’s darkness makes it difficult to muster power for mystical work; each success test for activating a mystical power or ritual gets a second chance to fail. If the effect occurs where the ceremony is performed, this applies to the area outside the circle.

14-14.description:
Wave of Corruption
A wave of corruptive energy emanates outward from the place where the ceremony is performed or takes effect. Everyone in the area immediately suffers [[2d6]] temporary corruption; everyone within a fifty-meter radius is also knocked unconscious unless they pass a Resolute test. They remain unconsciousness for one scene. If the effect occurs where the ceremony is performed, this applies to the area outside the circle.

15-15.description:
Echo of Corruption
The area’s infectious darkness echoes strongly, and all temporary corruption suffered by creatures remains until they have left the affected area.

16-16.description:
Corrupting Environment
The place reeks with corruption, and everyone in the area must pass a Strong test every hour or suffer 1d4 temporary corruption. The corruption does not leave the afflicted until he or she leaves the area. If the effect occurs where the ceremony is performed, this applies to the area outside the circle.

17-17.description:
Death Flees
Undeath, as in the race Undead, is spreading like the plague; the dead will not remain still, and rise with their minds intact.

18-18.description:
Unsound Temptation
The area gains the properties of Dark Davokar. If it is in Ambria, creatures which are otherwise found in Bright Davokar may be encountered there; if it is in Bright Davokar, the area turns into an enclave of Dark Davokar in terms of what monsters inhabit the place (see Travel & Encounters in the Core Rulebook to learn what monster can usually be found where).

19-19.description:
The Dead Awake
The buried dead claw their way out of their tombs and haunt the place at night (stats like Dragouls, in the Core Rulebook ). If any living person stays in the area, they will only survive the night behind reinforced walls and sealed gates.

20-20.description:
Corrupted Waters
The area’s groundwater becomes a source of corruption; those who drink from it suffer [[1d8]] temporary corruption with each drink. It is possible to visit the area without being affected, as long as one brings water from somewhere else.

1-1.description:
Event: A plague ravages the domain
Effect:
The plague adds a -[[1d6]] penalty to Management tests every season until it has been eradicated with a successful Cunning test from someone with the Medicus ability. One attempt per season is allowed, and Alchemy adds +1 to the test.

2-2.description:
Event: Brigands on the road
Effect:
The brigands hinder travel and trade, giving -[[1d4]] to Management tests every season until they are defeated or driven away.

3-3.description:
Event: Monsters in the area
Effect:
Monsters are wreaking havoc:

4-4.description:
Event: Crop failure
Effect:
Crop failure strikes the region for 1d8 seasons, during which time a -[[1d4]] penalty to all Management tests is in effect.

5-5.description:
Event: Riot
Effect:
It takes a successful Persuasive test to handle the riot; the abilities Dominate and Leader each add +1 to the test. Failure inflicts a -[[1d4]] penalty to the season’s Management test.

6-6.description:
Event: Serial killer on the loose
Effect:
A murderer is killing [[1d4-1]] victims per season. The killer must be stopped, otherwise a cumulative –1 penalty is added to each season’s Management test; in other words, after three seasons the modifier is –3, which is the maximum.

7-7.description:
Event: Gang on a robbing spree
Effect:
Until the gang is stopped, a cumulative –1 penalty is added to each season’s Management test; in other words, after three seasons the modifier is –3, which is the maximum.

8-8.description:
Event: Trade proposal
Effect:
A delegate shows up with a trade agreement that adds +1 to Management tests for [[1d4]] seasons.

9-9.description:
Event: Wave of refugees
Effect:
The refugees cause –1 to Management tests for [[1d8]] seasons, then 0 for [[1d8]] seasons, and finally the established refugees add a permanent +1 bonus to all Management tests.

10-10.description:
Event: Distinguished guests
Effect:
A wealthy group of people, perhaps a mercenary company or a traveling carnival troupe, boosts trade in the domain. Then they move on. The visit adds +1 to the season’s Management test.

11-11.description:
Event: Visiting liege lord
Effect:
The leader’s liege lord comes to visit accompanied by an entourage of warriors, advisors and courtiers. Such a visits calls for a feast, for princes and commoners alike. It adds +1 to the current season’s Management test, then –2 to the next one.

12-12.description:
Event: Call for aid
Effect:
A neighbouring domain is in crisis and requests assistance.

Heeding the call initially modifies the current season’s Management test by -1.

Then there is a 50% chance of the neighbor emerging from the crisis stronger and grateful for the help, adding +2 to the next season’s test.

13-13.description:
Event: Cultist infiltration
Effect:
Rumors of strange figures and unhuly meetings are circulating, and soon people start disappearing – mostly vagrants, homeless beggars and other down-and-out individuals.

As long as the cultists are influencing life in the domain, all Management tests are modified by -1.

The cultists are not easy to get rid of.

14-14.description:
Event: Settlers moving in
Effect:
The settlers give a 0 modifier to Management tests for [[1d8]] seasons, after which the established settlers add a permanent +1 bonus to all Management tests.

16-16.description:
Event: Bad omen
Effect:
Seers are reading bad omens; rull on the table until you get a negative event, which then occurs within [[1d4-1]] seasons.

17-17.description:
Event: Good sign
Effect:
Seers are seeing good signs; rull on the table until you get a positive event, which then occurs within [[1d4-1]] seasons.

18-18.description:
Event: Tempting treasure deposit
Effect:
A treasure deposit attracts fortune hunters to the area for [[1d6]] seasons; rull again if the result is 6; another 6 means that their stay is permanent. Their chaotic presence has a 50% chance of giving either +2 or –1 to Management tests for the duration of their stay.
